title: Simple Guide to Hedging with Options
text: What's up outliers? Today we're going to talk about hedging with options. This is hands down one of the most misunderstood topics with respect to retail options trading. And it's because retail traders have a really really messed up way of approaching hedging that leads to the worst possible outcome, which is where we're increasing our friction, our total cost, but also reducing our profitability. So, we're going to walk through some of the core concepts as to why that's the case. We're going to talk about some ways to hedge smarter, and then I'll give you a couple examples to help tie everything together. So, the base here, as you know, options are strategic investments. They're designed to create very specific riskreward profiles. They also allow you to speculate, so trade an idea that you have or hedge to reduce risk. So what is the concept of hedging in a nutshell? Well, that's it. It's literally just to reduce risk. I view it in two different concepts. The first way is a direct hedge. The second is indirect. These are not industry terms. These are my own. But it helps me organize the way I think about hedging. So when we think about a direct hedge, the entire purpose of the position in effect is to lose. Why would we put on a position that we want to lose?

title: Simple Guide to Hedging with Options
text: about hedging. So when we think about a direct hedge, the entire purpose of the position in effect is to lose. Why would we put on a position that we want to lose? Because the purpose of a direct hedge is to offset the risk of a primary position. And the preferred outcome would be for the primary position to win, which would mean that our hedge would lose. And if your mind immediately goes to, well, what about if I put on the hedge and then I make money on the hedge, but then I make money on the main position? Well, if you can solve that, you've solved the market. But more importantly, this is one of the big issues that retail traders have with the concept of hedgings because that's what they want to happen. But that's not how this works. And I'll explain why. An indirect hedge I'm putting it on to make money, but it happens to fit in with the broader portfolio and serve a risk reduction purpose as well. And I'll show you what that looks like.
